discussion: The skeleton was found in a pit. It is in a complete, tightly flexed position lying on the left side. The bones are all in articulation. The hyoid and the patellas are in position. Left hand located under the legs at the feet. The right foot is pressed against the limit of the pit, forced into an unnatural position. Scapula is displaced vertically pressed against the side of the pit. Right humerus has fragmented. The hand and arm bones are in correct anatomical position. The skull and the mandible are broken / smashed. Inclusions of the immediate grave soil occur as tiny flecks of charcoal but only a few. A white substance perhaps a form of clay occurred quite commonly. Very similar to this was a white fibrous material which was laid down in lines both vertically and horizontally (?) Plant root salts /phytoliths. No relative movement of the bones since internment. This skeleton may have been tied as the bones are so tightly flexed.

discussion: Articulated adult female skeleton. The skeleton is completely flexed, lying on its left side, head to the south. All bones are in articulation. There is some black stain inside the upper rib cage. The thoracic vertebrae also have a black staining. Almost all bones are in poor condition, because it was exposed some for time. Some consolidant (paraloid) was put on skull and right coxae. Sacrum is completely broken. All the bones on the left side are crushed by the weight of the body and soil. There is empty space in thoracic cage. The teeth are heavily worn and rotted. Cranial vault also empty. The left foot is under the other long bones from 1425 which is north-west side of the pit. On the mandible there are some remains of fibres, and also on the ischium. 2007-Sample taken for C14 (R ribs) BA (15/08/2011)
head: pointing
other_comments: the vertebral column is straight, without its round curve.
surface_modifications: Black staining on ribs 1-7 and Th 1-7

discussion: All the bones are in anatomical position. The skeleton is missing the skull and Atlas (first cervical vertebra). The other cervicals are in articulation though the Axis (second cervical vertebra) has lost its head. Some of the tarsal bones from the right foot are ossified and one of the medial phalanx show pathological modification. The body is orientated east-west and is located in the centre of the burial pit. 2007-sample taken for C14 (Right rib)-BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: A tightly contracted burial of a child / adolescent, in or just beneath SW platform of space 112. (Mellaart's shrine VII.9) The burial has its head to the ESE, into the room and feet close to the W wall of the building. It was found too close to the present surface for a cut to be visible but has clearly been squashed into a confined space (note the attitude of the head and hands). There was no evidence for binding but the presence of both patellas is indicative that the body was fleshed when interred. A clay ball fragment lay in front of the face - though this association could be fortuitous - see (1880). The presence of other burials under this platform is shown by a skull (burial 1885) immediately to the S. Ref for Shrine VII.9 is Mellaart 1964 (the 3rd prelim, report) pp 52-4 & plate Va. The report makes no mention of burials in this room.

discussion: Skeleton of a juvenile on its left side in a hocker position with its knees brought up under the chin. Traces of white phytoliths were uncovered near north and south sides of skull, and a second line on the south side near the legs, and skull - possibly remains of a basket in which body was placed. Round piece of yellow material (possibly ochre ?? but very unsure) found near knee. Similar material found beneath vertebrae, pelvis and ribs. Skeleton in south-east area of pit. * Bone removed but not planned (photographed) 12 left ribs 13 left scapula 14 left femur 15 skull 16 hand- phalanages & carpals (right) 17 radius and ulna (right) 18 clavical 19 left foot 20 left calcaneus 21 tibia (left) 22 fibula (left) 23 left hand Red paint found on right hand bones. Phytoliths around skull, near body/thorax, around left foot 2007-Sample taken for C14 (right tibia and left tibia)-BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: Adult skeleton lying on right side, head pointing into the centre of the room (south-east), tightly flexed, knees up to the chin, left arm going under left leg, right arm going over right leg i.e. both arms between thighs; right hand over left hand This is a fully articulated skeleton - it could represent the earliest burial under the platform, however although there were semi articulated bodies over it such as (1938) none are as complete - it is therefore possible it was inserted last in F.38 and the others piled back over in the fill; certainly the shape of the cut defines its body (1924) very well - all in all this is probably the latest burial in fact interred in F.38

discussion: Earliest burials, not disturbed by insertion (1466); however bone is still semi-articulated or disarticulated suggesting several/more than one re-cut/reopening of grave before (1466) disturbing bones - unless they were deposited in that condition although this appears unlikely given the way the bones are so frequently up against the edge of the deposit/cut and at upright angles, suggesting moving of bones out to the edge when inserting a new corpse

discussion: Scattered very young individual bones, presumably belong to same individual. The bones from the 2 burials are consistent in age, size and morphology, and on the same they are grouped together in 1935.

discussion: 96: Skeleton of a small child lying in north-east area of grave-pit - skeleton (1912) directly above , and skeletons of adult and child (burial 30) to the north-west. Skeleton (1950) is lying east-west with its head towards the west looking north. The right arm is straight down the body, and the left arm is lying across the chest. The legs are bent up. Similar yellow organic material found as was found with skeleton (1912). Skeleton covered up for excavation next year. 97: the body is complete except from sacrum, left cotae, left hand and right femur. it was sitting in a phytolith wrapping. The legs pushed to the bottom of the wrapping it is covering the shoulders. The burial is primary. Apart form the left foot bones, the general condition is good. The head is very fragmentary. Under the body, there was yellow ochre smeared to surface around the pelvis and lumbar vertebrae. 2007-Sample taken for C14 (L ulna, L femur)-BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: The eastern half of this burial was exposed for some time, but the western part was only exposed after burials 38 and 42 were removed. Unfortunately, during this time the western part of the burial became damp during a rainstorm, and this did not aid the condition of the bone! Basak, Peter and Gavin all seemed to agree (as did I) that the western crushed skull and the majority of the post cranial bones were all from the same individual (prior to lab analysis). To the east, tucked under the overhang of the cut, was the crushed skull of another individual, possibly a juvenile or subadult (skull was smaller and thinner). This may have been pushed to one side when the adult inhumation was inserted. It was not clear during excavation what post-cranial bones (if any) were associated with this skull. A third skull fragment lay to the south. This was of a baby/small child. Some of the small bones that were removed during the cleaning up of the larger bones probably belonged to this child. The relationship between this inhumation and the main body was not clear -- no different grave cut was visible. The green stone pendant/bead 1955.x1 was associated with the main adult bones, and was tucked in between a femur and the pelvis. It was not clear if this was its original position or not. Most of the long bones had been penetrated by fine rootlets and when lifted they just collapsed into fragments. The dampness of the bones also made them very delicate. To separate bones out slightly, the juvenile skull was given a separate unit number (2125). Similarly, during the initial lifting of 1955, infant bones began appearing and these were assigned a new unit number too (2126). These infant bones were located roughly underneath the patch of yellow ochre (ooer!). During lifting, it proved impossible to keep the cranium together as a solid block. The skull had been compressed along the suture lines, and because there was a partial void in the soil matrix within, the block had no internal stability. However, it was still possible to keep some integrity of shape. The crushed vertebrae just disintegrated into fragments when I attempted to lift them, as did the pelvis and sacrum. Pelvis and sacrum were lying immediately on top of the foot bones of 2169. The spine of 1955 lay across the leg bones of 1955. No bone of 2169 was truncated by 1955's grave cut (2190) however, and this must be deliberate. The very bottom of 2190 must have been excavated with extreme care. This, and the way that 1955 was flexed around not only to fit in cut but also to have an intimate and close physical relationship to 2169 may indicate that 2169 individual known or important to 1955 -- same kin, lineage etc. The skull of 1955 was also resting on the left side of 1969's skull. The previous discussion is now flawed, as the human bone team identified hand bones belonging to this individual below the skeleton on 2169. 1955 was therefore probably the primary burial beneath the platform F.13, lying in an undetected 'ghost' cut numbered as 2536. This was disturbed by 2176, now seen as a re-cutting episode, after which 2169 was interred in the cleaned out grave cut, along with two limb bones of 1955 (numbered as 2506). A flat-bottomed revetment was then cut into the northern edge of the grave cut, before 1955 was redeposited across the chronologically later burial of 2169. There was a clear flat soil interface between grave fills 2124 and 2175 to suggest this action, and the cut interface of 2190 itself. As suggested previously, there appears to have been a need to make a close physical link between 2169 and 1955, although also a desire for at least some separation (why did they not put 1955 back in the same cut as 2169 for example?). 2 long bones were removed from a (presumably?) well-rotted/bare skeleton 1955 and put (perhaps bound in a bundle) in with the later burial, which was then covered by the primary skeleton. Was this tow related individuals being linked after death, or perhaps one individual seeking to draw on the status/prestige/authority (etc., etc.!)of an earlier known ancestor/relative/kin group member? 1955 was obviously moved with a fair degree of care. It now appears that (1955) includer two separate depositional events, one of which is part of F.47 and the other is in F.204 and an attempt has been made to split these retrospectively. CC 01-02-01 08/09/09 It has been determined at this juncture that the remains of this skeleton found in F.47 were the result of disturbance of the primary burial F.204 and not deliberately placed. This means that this skeleton has been removed from F.47. BB

discussion: This collection of bones (burial 42B) lay underneath the skull and neck vertebrae of burial 42A. However, as these 2 groups of bones are not necessarily from he same individual, they were treated as 2 separate burials in the filed (though lab work may indeed confirm they were from the same person.) The bones consisted of at least one femur, foot bones (tarsals) and some ribs, & one tooth (identified in the field that is) These were arranged in a tight linear grouping orientated E-W with the grave cut, and sloping gently to the east. This tight arrangement suggests that although the bones were disarticulated they may have been placed with some structure and care. The size of the bones indicates a possible juvenile / child. The bones are in a very poor condition - they were not only brittle, but also crushed by the presence from above. Few (if any) were undamaged. The relatively regular nature of the cut and the placement of the bones all suggest a deliberate act of inhumation rather than the mere disposal of bones disturbed by later interments. Some salt deposits had formed around these bones.

raw_text_excerpt: Çatalhöyük Research Project: On-line Database FacilityCatalhoyuk Research Project: On-line Database Facility Skip to main content English Visit Us Contact Home The Site The Project Research Portal Partners Unit 1961 Category: skeleton dug in 1997 Area: North Interpretive Categories: skeleton Data Category Information: none Dimensions: est 0.5 x 0.25m Discussion: This collection of bones (burial 42B) lay underneath the skull and neck vertebrae of burial 42A. However, as these 2 groups of bones are not necessarily from he same individual, they were treated as 2 separate burials in the filed (though lab work may indeed confirm they were from the same person.) The bones consisted of at least one femur, foot bones (tarsals) and some ribs, & one tooth (identified in the field that is) These were arranged in a tight linear grouping orientated E-W with the grave cut, and sloping gently to the east. This tight arrangement suggests that although the bones were disarticulated they may have been placed with some structure and care. The size of the bones indicates a possible juvenile / child. The bones are in a very poor condition - they were not only brittle, but also crushed by the presence from above. Few (if any) were undamaged. The relatively regular nature of the cut and the placement of the bones all suggest a deliberate act of inhumation rather than the mere disposal of bones disturbed by later interments. Some salt deposits had formed around these bones. discussion: Articulated skeleton of a baby / infant. Child is lying on its back with its legs tucked up in front of it and pointing north, though the top of the skull is crushed and has split along it suture lines, the post-cranial bones are relatively well preserved. During the initial cleaning of the skeleton, Basak noticed several small beads, there proved to be dozens (and ultimately hundreds) of those small beads, all approx. 3 mm wide (max!). Most had a polished blue-black outer surface, but 3 white shell examples and 1 red (coral?) beads were also recovered. It was initially thought that these were anklet bracelets as they were concentrated around the left and right tibia and fibia, but subsequent excavation discovered clusters of beads around the right knee, and on top of the right ribs. Three strings of beads were discovered running across the right humerus and looping around where the neck would have been. Either these beads were sewn onto the child’s clothing (if it was buried in any), or they were part of a long necklace which was looped around the body and limbs of the baby / infant. This latter option seems the most likely. They were also below the right tibia and fibia. When the skeleton was partially excavated, the ribs, spine and pelvic bones were all revealed. Two rows of beads were lying beneath the left ribs, including at least three white limestone / coral examples which were together on their row. The beads on the right of the skull were actually lying on top of the right scapula. Those lying across the right ribs had actually slipped in between the rib bones, presumably as the flesh and or any clothing rotted away. Several sketches of skeleton with beads in situ included with this unit sheet. AMC 19/8/97: Description with isometric projections: Based on this I would argue that 2 strings at least were wrapped around the shoulders and neck, whilst a third string went from around the shoulder down to the ankles, possibly after being looped around the right knee. One long looped bead string should have been capable of this. 2007-Sample taken for C14 (left femur)-BA (15/08/2011)

raw_text_excerpt: Çatalhöyük Research Project: On-line Database FacilityCatalhoyuk Research Project: On-line Database Facility Skip to main content English Visit Us Contact Home The Site The Project Research Portal Partners Unit 2105 Category: skeleton dug in 1997 Area: North Related Photo (Click to view larger version in new window) Interpretive Categories: skeleton Data Category Information: none Dimensions: 0.35 m long, 0.20 m wide Discussion: Articulated skeleton of a baby / infant. Child is lying on its back with its legs tucked up in front of it and pointing north, though the top of the skull is crushed and has split along it suture lines, the post-cranial bones are relatively well preserved. During the initial cleaning of the skeleton, Basak noticed several small beads, there proved to be dozens (and ultimately hundreds) of those small beads, all approx. 3 mm wide (max!). Most had a polished blue-black outer surface, but 3 white shell examples and 1 red (coral?) beads were also recovered. It was initially thought that these were anklet bracelets as they were concentrated around the left and right tibia and fibia, but subsequent excavation discovered clusters of beads around the right knee, and on top of the right ribs. Three strings of beads were discovered running across the right humerus and looping around where the neck would have been. Either these beads were sewn onto the child’s clothing (if it was buried in any), or they were part of a long necklace which was looped around the body and limbs of the baby / infant. This latter option seems the most likely. They were also below the right tibia and fibia. When the skeleton was partially excavated, the ribs, spine and pelvic bones were all revealed. Two rows of beads were lying beneath the left ribs, including at least three white limestone / coral examples which were together on their row. The beads on the right of the skull were actually lying on top of the right scapula. Those lying across the right ribs had actually slipped in between the rib bones, presumably as the flesh and or any clothing rotted away. Several sketches of skeleton with beads in situ included with this unit sheet. AMC 19/8/97: Description with isometric projections: Based on this I would argue that 2 strings at least were wrapped around the shoulders and neck, whilst a third string went from around the shoulder down to the ankles, possibly after being looped around the right knee. One long looped bead string should have been capable of this.

discussion: Crouched / flexed burial of adult lying in left side facing N, head to centre of room. It is completely articulated and undisturbed, although the spine of skeleton (1995) immediately overlay the skull, distorting the spine, which suggests (2115) must have been uncovered in antiquity. The distal end of the left femur is very high, lying on an earth ridge, which suggests that if this is indeed part of a double burial along infill 2119., each skeleton had a separate lower scoop in the cut within which it was placed. Organic / sooty deposits in the ribcage was very heavy & may be from lungs. Yellow ochre and pieces of grey pigments in the waist area and the fill are of interest

discussion: The group of baby / infant semi-articulated bones lay below some of the adult bones of skeleton 1955 but they were also mixed up with many of the adult bones and there was no clear stratigraphic relationship between them, These baby/infant bones were badly crushed, and due to their delicate nature few bones survived lifting intact. The body of the infant (if there had ever been a whole one) may have been placed on a north-south orientation across the more E-W body of an adult (1955). Rotting and post-depositional processes then ensured that the infant bones settled down through and became co-mingled with 1955. On the other hand, the lack of obvious cranial remains has to be explained. After lifting of the ribs, infant long bones were uncovered which were sketched above and then removed. Subsequent cleaning and removal of some of 1955's rib elements revealed that most (if not all) of 2125's post cranial remains had in fact survived. As previously thought, the spread of ribs (now possibly the left rather than the right side) had detached themselves from the core of the skeleton and had slumped sideways to the W. The position of the vertebrae, pelvic bones and limb bones on the second plan 97/173 suggest that the infant was placed on its left side in a crouched position, pelvis to the North. Foot bones from 2125 lay on the right cheek and jaw of the adult 1955 skull, and further foot bones lay just to he SE of this skull. Hand bones lay in groups to the South of the adult skull and also across the vertebrae of 2125, and also to the East of the adult skull. I seems likely that the right arm and hand lay on top of the upper (right) side of the body therefore. BUT (presumably a later addition) Basak then discovered that the 2nd lot of infant bones were largely complete and semi-articulated. The original set of ribs must therefore have belonged to a separate individual. This was renumbered as 2168. It also includes arm bones 2007-Sample taken for C14 (left femur, left ulna) BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: This disarticulated skull was the highest skeletal element in burial 47. Its smallish size and thin cranial bone thickness led Basak to suggest that it is a juvenile. It lay tucked underneath the undercut north eastern edge of the grave cut. Though no clear stratigraphic relationship between this skull and the adult bones of unit (1955) could be established (and so both have been put as being below fill 1982 and above fill 2124), it is probable that this skull was placed in the burial after some if not all of the adult bones had been deposited. An adult femur from skeleton 1955 was partially overlain by the skull. However, skull 2126 may have been earlier, and pushed to one side as 1955 was being inserted. During such a process, the femur could easily have been shoved underneath the earlier skull. The relationship between them must thus remain equivocal. No clearly identifiable juvenile post-cranial remains could be identified during excavation, lab work may do so. When this skull was lifted, it simply collapsed into a heap of barely articulating fragments. Not only had numerous small rootlets penetrated around, behind and through the skull, but a small mammal burrow had penetrated underneath the skull and had further disturbed the cranial bones. The left ribs of skeleton 1955 were lying behind 2126 to the north and north east.

discussion: Partially articulated skeleton of a young infant. Truncated heavily to South by burial 202 and to North by burial 47. Body itself lies in a small cut (2507), which is a small undercut niche on extreme east of platform. Bones of 2141 lie on top of the disarticulated gracile adult disarticulated long bones of 2506 Skull of 2141 is largely missing, and mandibles are badly smashed. Body lay with back on a curving N-S axis, with its head to the south and its pelvis and legs to the North. very tightly crouched. Most of the bones were lifted as a block to try and aid preservation of bones. (added p2 17.9.97?) Rather than pre-dating 1955, the skeleton probably post-dated its original deposition, but was then probably truncated by re-cut 2176. However, as I did not understand this at he time of excavation, any definite relationship this burial had to earlier or later burials cannot now be proven. Its bones did sit on 2506 however, so they may post-date the insertion of burial 204, but pre-date the redeposition of 1955. this would imply a very quick episode between burial 204 and 47, which seems implausible. 2007-Sample taken for C14 (R ulna, R femur) BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: Flexed / crouched inhumation, fully articulated. Very robust adult (probably male so Basak and Theya say), lying in a tightly crouched position with its spine curved along a rough E-W axis. Feet are tucked up tightly under overhang, soles facing east (as does face of skull). Skull is badly crushed, and some of long bones are also cracked due to compression, and also root penetration. A pronounced void was encountered during excavation, located between the sternum and chin. A piece of shell was located between lower mandible and patellas. A linear concave 'furrow' or depression ran obliquely across the surface of the right ribs, and this represented a linear fracture caused by downward pressure exerted on the right humerus from above. When lifting the bones, the proximal ends of many disintegrated, as these were already crushed. However, the bone as a whole was in much better condition than skeleton 1955, unfortunately, hairline cracks in the pelvis caused it to collapse, and for this reason I decided to lift the bulk of the torso and vertebrae as a block. A small patch of yellow ochre was discovered beneath the left ribs, this was sampled. (3rd page added 17.9.97) On previous pages I outlined what I thought were the sequences of events involved in this individual's burial. However, the human bone team discovered hand bones belonging to 1955 under skeleton 2169. This means that 1955 was probably the primary inhumation under the platform F13. It's grave (a 'ghost' cut of which only traces remained - see sheet 2176) is now numbered as 2536. This inhumation was then removed by 2176, which should now be seen as a re-cut, and then 2169 was deposited in this cut and the grave filled in. Two long bones belong to 1955 (2506) were also included in this burial. 1955's partially disarticulated skeleton was then reburied on top of 2169, so that it appeared to be stratigraphically earlier. See sheet 1955 for a discussion of why this may have been desired. 2007-Sample taken C14 (Right rib) BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: Small group of disarticulated bones in burial 204. Some rib fragments and (hand?) bones were present , possibly forma juvenile / infant, as epiphyses were unfused. Probably represent disarticulated bones disturbed / pushed to one side before / during insertion of 2195 in grave cut. However, as they were physically on top of the fill, it is possible that they may relate to 2169, the group of infant disarticulated bones sat on top of 2125. On the other hand, these may relate to the long bones and / or other elements of 2506

discussion: Many voids were apparent when excavating this neonate, both within the ribcage and around the arms, suggesting organic decay - possible food between the arms? - or flowers?. Also the right arm has dropped some 3 cm or more below the clavicle, suggesting again possible decay of something, or else a strange disturbance of the clavicle. Excellent preservation of the bones, perfect articulation of a hand and foot. Three possible grave goods, to be examined later. This burial was cut into the lower sub-floor packing and respects the lowest course of the W wall of space 71. Next to it is another neonate - or premature - baby in a similar cut. These may be foundation burials. Lots of chalk in the fill. Possible phytolith around base - textile? When scapula (R) was lifted, the soil below looked seed-rich (<3>). Second rib in the main group was on its side, flat - why? (30.8) 1.9: found another clavicle in the right position - I thought the other was a bit big / odd (but no left clavicle so far) 14/09/09 Michele Massa: I have deleted x-finds in 2199 since they are repetition of x-finds in 2194

other_comments: Fully articulated neonate in crouched position lying on left side to wall, facing north. Seems to be twisted slightly onto its back, from position of L arm. R clavicle very high above arm.

discussion: Both patella's are present, but both have shifted slightly. Both lower legs are in reasonable condition. Both feet are present, but jumbled making their position impossible to determine. Though they probably both extended Eastwards. There are iron nail throughout the grave, and one lay between the skeletons ankles. A glass? token was found next to the head on the North side, a glass vial was next to the head on the South side. The head and upper torso appear to have sunk somewhere into the midden below - large animal burrows were directly under the human bones. A large rock sat below the skeleton's feet. The skeleton's long bones did not survive removal very well, but the rest of it survived with only minor to moderate damage. We had to cut into the midden below to remove the skull.
body: Body lies extended and supine, with its head to the West and its feet to the East. The centre of the torso is quite disarranged and damaged - some ribs and vertebrae are out of place.
head: Is slumped forward looking South at its own right shoulder. A roughly triangular piece of skull is missing from the forehead over the right eye., and another portion of the skull is missing at the back.
right_arm: Lower part of the humurus missing truncated by animal burrow. Lower part of arm present but damaged lying alongside the body. Right hand is mostly gone, only a few bones remain next to the femur.
left_arm: Lower part of the humurus missing truncated by animal burrow. Lower part of arm present but damaged lying alongside the body. Left hand is damaged and lies next to the hip.
left_leg: Extended. Left femur is mostly intact.
surface_modifications: None noticable upon excavation, although grave fill included a lot of burnt material and a bit of burnt horn core in the eyesocket (residual?).

discussion: This a juvenile burial probably dating to the Roman period which is associated with burial 1 (f.150) and burial 2 (F.151). The burial cuts throught the Neolithic levels for approx. 35-40 cm. The skeleton appears to have been in a coffin because several iron nails were found in the fill. Several grave goods are associated with this burial including 2 bone tools, a glass vial and a copper bead necklace ( see U.2226). The skeleton is in fairly good shape with most of its bones intact.

discussion: This skeleton was in poor condition and many of the bones disintegrated upon removal. The left arm bones and mandible were in particularly poor shape. We left the lower legs and feet in place because they are partially cut off by the baulk. We'll try to leave them in until we can remove them without damaging them more than necessary. We cut through the baulk to get to the lower legs and feet and found the left foot in fairly good condition (most of the bones seem to be present), while the right foot bones are out of place and muddled.
body: This is an extended, supine skeleton lying roughly east-west (head to west), although the feet angle slightly south. This burial is slightly angled compared to the other graves.
head: The head is slumped down and forward, facing slightly to the left (north).
right_arm: The right arm is straight along the side, with the hand resting on the pelvis. Most hand bones are missing as is the upper arm.
other_comments: The skeleton is fairly weathered - many of the bones will deteriorate upon removal.

discussion: This skeleton was in fair condition generally except the skull was crushed before excavation and broke apart when we tried to remove it in a block. Like the skeleton in F.153 the tibia are cut by the baulk. We left them in for removal later. The skeleton shows signs of a high degree of post depositional disturbance. Some of the ribs were poking out of the profile, 5-10 cm above the rest of the skeleton and 15-20 cm out of place. Rodents are the most likely cause of this although we haven't seen obvious rodent holes in the cut. The burial appears to have slumped down into the midden below a couple of cm's particularly in the central part of the body. Excavation of the lower part of the burial (east of the baulk) produced a few foot bones, as well as a clear rodent hole. 08/10/98 - During excavation in the baulk we encountered a ring (a toe ring?). It is green, could be copper or bone. It was marked as X1 in the fill (2235). As well as X2 a circular iron object. The section of the skeleton under the baulk was excavated in '98. Some more bones were recovered along with 2 rings and a nail. The bones were mostly of the left leg and the feet. We have almost the whole lower left leg and left foot. No ther toes were found. The bones were in a very fragile condition and some of them fragmented on removal.

left_leg: Extended. Lower leg and foot is mostly under the baulk.
other_comments: Most of the ribs are displaced. There are two ribs on the southern wall of the cut and on the side of the left leg.

discussion: Though this burial has a very clear cut the skeleton seems to be disturbed. There were two other burials on top of this one (.. .. & .. ..) so that may be the reason for its poor condition. Left arm is detached from the body, lying on the left side of the body. The left leg is detached as well. The vertebral column seems to be disturbed, with a gap between the thoracic & ?lower lumbar vertebrae (see plan). Some ribs were lying under the left hand, suggesting the chest had been crushed & spread out. The right arm is in the right place but a bit far from the body. The head was smashed and no featured could be identified.

discussion: This number was assigned to the 2 adult gracile long bones that = lying beneath skeletons 2169 and 2141, and = tucked up against eastern edge of burial 207 (grave cut 2176). A few disarticulated bones, mostly from the southern edge of the grave cut, were also included within this context. These long bones = likely to represent the primary burial beneath platform F. 13, pushed to one side when 2169 = interred. However, it is possible that these bones might relate to skull 2126 -- the juvenile cranium, and perhaps also/instead the collection of bones numbered as 2195. Only lab work could confirm this, but if this is the case, then the stratigraphic sequence may have to be rethought. *These = matched to 1955 -- see that skeleton sheet's continuation for a discussion of this.

discussion: Semi-articulated skeleton of an infant, disturbed almost certainly by and animal burrow which runs directly through the centre of the burial. However, the body is in a very doubled up position but while legs and arms as far as one can tell, more or less extended. This is very possibly a baby-in-a-bag, although no phytoliths were observed - however now as there a distinctive cut and slight soil differences noted on the surface could be due to the animal burrow. Several bones clearly missing which must be attributed to the animal. 2007-sample taken for C14 (R ulna L femur) BA (15/08/2011)

discussion: This group of foot bones was exposed (and damaged!) when mattocking out the primary makeup deposit (2514) of the 'platform' feature F.13. No other bones were present, and close examination of the soil matrix the bones were in (2521) showed that this deposit though partially derived from platform material, must have been a grave fill remnant ignored/missed by the excavator in the 1996 season. As these bones and 2521 were located at the western end of the platform, the burial the bones most likely relate to = burial 38_. However, this is by no means certain.

discussion: tight crouched, fully articulated yet some limbs seem to have been wrenched out of place, e.g. L femur, L ulna. Left hand has dropped to a peculiar vertical position, and all limbs except L arm are extremely tightly flexed. Many voids were faunal in the surrounding soil, not just in the rib cage but around arms, legs, feet. Pelvis and feet very close to surface and rather damaged as a result. Many traces of phytolith - textile shroud or mat all around & below? skeleton, pieces on pelvis & L scapula; also dense grasses overlying the ribs. The L hand was way down by R elbow, with bones going below R femur. Heavy sooty black deposit with white layer on it, inside ribs, lying on R ribs. I feel this body had been dead some time before being buried - tied up tight in a bundle. this permitted some bones, e.g. femur & ulna, to pull out of position, & L hand to drop, partly articulated, below R leg. R hand also in odd position. No sign of animal disturbance to account for this, nor does it follow pattern of root disturbance. Ribs crushed together also.

left_leg: knee very low by chin, steeply angled with foot on pelvis,
other_comments: general suggestion on very tight binding & body not being buried imediately, hence joints outof place,
surface_modifications: Very badly damaged by roots, although they appear good at first sight

discussion: Infant burial at NW-SE alignment, with head at SE end. Extended and on its back, legs splayed, right leg bent and knee flat whilst left leg slightly bent at knee and raised, feet almost touching. Head appears to have been pushed down over chest, which has resulted in spinal column curved towards the east (not sure whether it is disarticulated). Between the ankles is a stone (2771, X1). The right upper arm lies along torso with the lower arm and hand below the right thigh. The left upper arm lay below the rib cage with elbow bent and upper arm and hand on the rib cage (nice touch, hand over the heart!). Very occasional fragments of phytoliths, suggesting of being bound or trussed in matting, but no real evidence for this.
head: appears to have been disarticulated, skull lies crushed in rib cavity, mandible almost between the knees, no it would seem the head was pushed down not disarticulated.
left_arm: left arm humerus lay below the ribs, bent at elbow with hand on the ribcage (hand on the heart!)

discussion: Skeleton 2779 seems to be a burial in room fill 2776. It was in poor condition. All the bones were smashed and seemed to be disturbed. Some bones were not found at all, maybe they were but not identified. In general the head seems to be located in SW while the feet are in the NE. I could not find the face, but parts of mandible came out under the skull so its face was probably facing NE. From traces, the legs and arms seemed to be flexed but are no longer articulated very well and because of the damage it is hard to see.
head: see

discussion: Upon excavation of skeleton 2886, elements of the child skeleton 2842 that had not previously been revealed were found scattered over the torso and ribs of the adult 2886 (rib fragments vertebral bodies, left scapula). This suggests that the child 2842 was buried before the adult 2886 because elements from a single side of the child's body were disturbed, this side being nearest to the cut of the adult skeleton. The adult burial was completely undisturbed and is full articulated.
body: Articulated child skeleton in flexed position, lying on its right side.
head: Completely crushed and there is a shell near face.
right_arm: Straight upwards and hand is fallen down.
other_comments: There is some phytolith fragment on top of the legs. The phytolith doesn't have a shape. There is a stone bowl and a shell with red ocre next to face.

discussion: Clay figurine fragment (?) behind thoracic vertebrae 11-12, between vertebrae and ribs. How did it get there? Possibly placed on top of vertebral column and fell down through soil after decomposition of soft tissue. Shell directly under skull. This was excavated as earlier than Burial 274, however in post-excavation and indeed during excavation it became apparent that Burial 277 was later, seen due to the displacement of burial 274 which was partially beneath burial 277 too.
body: Very tightly contracted with knees to chin. Lies on left side.
head: Squashed from pressure of earth. On left side. Inarticulation with vertabrae.
right_arm: In articulation. Arms lie under legs.
left_arm: In articulation. Arms lie under legs but over left tibia with hand at feet.
right_leg: In articulation. Tightly contracted with knees to chin.
left_leg: In articulation. Tightly contracted with knees to chin.
other_comments: The body is in full articulation. Old brakes on the left tibia, mandibul and skull, both radii and ulnae. Probably as a result of weight of soil on skeleton. Skeleton is that of a mature male. Salt and charcoal in matrix surrounding skeleton.
surface_modifications: Some black manganese staning on all elements of the skeleton.
